pi_items.source: FDA approval records and multiple medical databases (Mayo Clinic, NIH, Wikipedia) confirm Bortezomib (brand name Velcade) is an FDA-approved proteasome inhibitor approved in May 2003 for multiple myeloma treatment

FDA approval records and multiple medical databases (Amgen, NIH, CancerNetwork) confirm Carfilzomib (brand name Kyprolis) is an FDA-approved proteasome inhibitor approved in July 2012 for relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma treatment
Green tea is a widely used natural substance containing catechins and EGCG (epigallocatechin gallate), documented in scientific literature and multiple medical sources

Drugs.com drug interaction database lists green tea with bortezomib as a MAJOR (contraindicated) interaction - 'Green tea decreases effects of bortezomib, and it is contraindicated'. Multiple peer-reviewed sources (PMC, PubMed, American Society of Hematology Blood Journal) document that green tea polyphenols (EGCG) block bortezomib's protease inhibitory function by binding directly to the boronic acid structure, preventing its anticancer effectiveness

No documented interaction between carfilzomib and green tea found in clinical trials, FDA safety data, or peer-reviewed literature. This is consistent with the pharmacological mechanism: EGCG from green tea interacts specifically with the boronic acid moiety in bortezomib through formation of a covalent cyclic boronate (documented in multiple sources including Drugs.com and PMC articles). Carfilzomib is an epoxyketone-based proteasome inhibitor (structurally different from bortezomib's boronic acid structure), and EGCG interactions are specific to boronic acid-based inhibitors, not epoxyketone-based inhibitors

Verified through drug interaction data and pharmacological mechanism. Bortezomib has a documented major/contraindicated interaction with green tea due to its boronic acid structure that can bind to EGCG polyphenols, potentially neutralizing the drug's anticancer effects. Carfilzomib lacks this interaction due to its epoxyketone structure. Therefore, carfilzomib is the safer option for patients who consume green tea
Verified by reading setup_task.sh and export_result.sh scripts which define OUTPUT_FILE=/sdcard/green_tea_safety_report.txt

privileged_info_summary: The task uses the real Liverpool Cancer Drug Interactions Database to compare drug-drug interactions. Bortezomib (Velcade) and Carfilzomib (Kyprolis) are both real, FDA-approved proteasome inhibitor drugs used to treat multiple myeloma. Green tea is a real substance containing EGCG polyphenols. According to Drugs.com and peer-reviewed literature, Bortezomib has a documented MAJOR/contraindicated interaction with green tea because EGCG polyphenols directly bind to bortezomib's boronic acid structure, blocking its protease inhibitory function and reducing its anticancer effectiveness. In contrast, Carfilzomib has no documented interaction with green tea because it is an epoxyketone-based inhibitor (not boronic acid-based), and the EGCG-green tea interaction is specific to boronic acid-based proteasome inhibitors. Therefore, Carfilzomib is the safer option for patients consuming green tea.
